document.source_title: The United States Is Repeating Its Silicon Mistake with Gallium Nitride
document.summary: The article discusses the critical role of gallium nitride in modern semiconductor technology and highlights the United States' vulnerabilities in this sector due to reliance on foreign production, particularly from China. It draws parallels between the current situation with gallium nitride and past mistakes made with silicon semiconductors, emphasizing the need for a robust domestic supply chain and manufacturing capabilities. The piece argues for a strategic pivot towards heterogeneous integration to ensure national security and technological leadership.
key_takeaways: China controls 99% of the world's primary gallium and has imposed export restrictions.
The U.S. National Defense Stockpile had zero gallium reserves when China banned exports.
The U.S. risks repeating past mistakes made with silicon semiconductors in gallium nitride production.
Gallium nitride is essential for modern radar and electronic warfare systems.
The U.S. must build a domestic supply chain for gallium nitride to avoid strategic vulnerabilities.
Investment in dual-use advanced packaging facilities is crucial for national security.
document.why_it_matters: The article underscores the importance of developing a domestic supply chain for gallium nitride to maintain U.S. technological leadership and national security. It highlights the risks of over-reliance on foreign production, particularly from adversarial nations, and advocates for strategic investments in manufacturing capabilities to prevent repeating historical failures in semiconductor production.

claims.claim_text: China controls 99 percent of the world’s primary gallium.
claims.evidence_text: China controls 99 percent of the world’s primary gallium, a critical mineral and semiconductor crucial for building the microchips of the future.

claims.claim_text: The U.S. National Defense Stockpile had zero gallium reserves when China imposed its December 2024 ban.
claims.evidence_text: The U.S. National Defense Stockpile had zero gallium reserves when that ban landed.

claims.claim_text: The U.S. risks repeating past mistakes made with silicon semiconductors.
claims.evidence_text: The United States has been here before... If similar blind spots persist, the United States risks repeating this failure with gallium nitride.

entities.name: China
entities.description: China is the dominant producer of gallium, controlling 99% of the world's primary gallium supply.
entities.evidence_text: China controls 99 percent of the world’s primary gallium.

entities.name: Taiwan Semiconductor Manufacturing Company
entities.description: A leading semiconductor foundry that controls nearly 70% of the global foundry market.
entities.evidence_text: As of 2024, Taiwan Semiconductor Manufacturing Company controls nearly 70 percent of the global foundry market.

entities.name: CHIPS Act
entities.description: A U.S. initiative aimed at boosting domestic semiconductor production.
entities.evidence_text: The 2022 Creating Helpful Incentives to Produce Semiconductors and Science (CHIPS) Act was a response to these vulnerabilities.

events.event: China placed export controls on gallium.
events.evidence_text: In 2023, it placed export controls on gallium to retaliate against American restrictions on the export of advanced chips to China.

events.event: China escalated to an outright ban on gallium exports to the U.S.
events.evidence_text: In December 2024, China escalated to an outright ban on gallium exports to the United States.

events.event: China temporarily suspended the gallium export ban.
events.evidence_text: The ban was temporarily suspended in November 2025 following a U.S.-China trade truce.
The Trump administration’s February 2026 launch of Project Vault — a $12 billion public-private initiative to stockpile critical minerals.

metrics.name: Gallium production capacity increase
metrics.evidence_text: China’s gallium production capacity rose from 20 to 900 metric tons of output between 2000 and 2025.

metrics.name: Gallium reserves in U.S. National Defense Stockpile
metrics.evidence_text: The U.S. National Defense Stockpile had zero gallium reserves when that ban landed.

metrics.name: Subsidies received by Intel
metrics.evidence_text: Intel received approximately $7.8 billion in subsidies and still laid off around 35,500 workers in less than two years.
